source.title: The effect of specific locomotor experiences on infants' avoidance behaviour on real and water cliffs

confidence_rationale: Well-powered cross-sectional study (N=102) directly testing how experience with different locomotor postures (crawling vs walking) shapes affordance-guided avoidance behavior. The posture-specificity finding -- that crawling experience, but not walking experience, predicts drop-off avoidance in walkers -- is directly relevant to INV-073 (exploration epoch must cover all action modalities) and INV-055 (harm/benefit geography accumulation is posture-specific). The additional finding that longer crawling experience accelerates recalibration to walking posture suggests a cumulative exploration benefit. Confidence at 0.70: study is cross-sectional, uses a specific and dramatic affordance (drop-off avoidance), and does not address the reward/benefit side of INV-055's valence map requirement.
failure_signatures: Affordance learning is posture-specific and does not transfer across locomotor mode: the cliff avoidance learned through crawling only partially transfers to walking, requiring active recalibration. REE's infant stage would need to expose the agent across its full action repertoire, not just a single locomotor policy.
The paper addresses harm avoidance only (drop-off geography), not benefit discovery. REE's INV-055 requires both harm/benefit geography accumulation -- the benefit side is unaddressed here.
Cross-sectional design: causal relationships between crawling experience and affordance learning are inferred, not experimentally verified.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Crawling experience duration is positively associated with drop-off avoidance in both crawlers and walkers. Walkers who had more crawling experience show better recalibration of drop-off avoidance than those with less. Longer crawling experience facilitates faster recalibration to the new walking capability. Crucially, posture-specific experience (crawling) transfers partially but not fully to a different posture (walking): the harm geography requires re-learning with each new locomotor modality.
mapping.ree_translation: INV-055 specifies that the infant stage must accumulate harm/benefit geography through novelty-driven exploration. Burnay et al. provide direct evidence that harm geography is encoded in a posture-specific, action-mode-specific manner -- consistent with the idea that REE's harm residue field must be populated through the specific action-observation pairs the agent uses in its later deliberate-planning phase. If the infant stage uses only random-walk exploration but deliberate planning uses CEM-generated action sequences, the harm geography accumulated during exploration may require recalibration. INV-073's exploration epoch requirement is strengthened: not just quantity of experience but diversity of action modalities during exploration is necessary.
mapping.mapping_caveat: This study addresses only harm avoidance (drop-offs) -- the benefit/reward side of the geography is not addressed. The mapping to REE's residue field is plausible but requires that REE's harm/benefit residue operates analogously to the posture-specific perceptual calibration described here, which is not guaranteed by current substrate design.
mapping.source_context: Human infants 11-16 months, 58 crawlers and 44 walkers, cross-sectional, visual cliff / water cliff apparatus. New Zealand / Portugal research groups.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality good: peer-reviewed, adequate N, Developmental Science, uses validated cliff paradigm. Mapping fidelity moderate: harm geography component of INV-055 is addressed; benefit component is not. Transfer risk moderate: locomotor cliff avoidance maps to REE's harm residue field plausibly but not identically.
